schools.summary: The dawn ratification of the illegal night trial (Luke 22:66); Jesus bound on arrest (John 18:12) and again now; the Sanhedrin's morning session (Lightfoot); full account in Matthew 27:1.
Bede: they could not even find a false charge but 'King of the Jews'; Theophylact: they gave Jesus to Rome but were themselves given to Rome (Psalm 28:5).
Henry: the unwearied industry of the wicked, who lost no time; the great sacrifice bound with cords (Psalm 118:27).

themes: the Lamb led to the Gentile judge

schools.summary: Pilate notices only the political charge; 'Thou sayest' an affirmation, the one question on which Christ's testimony was essential; the fuller interview in John 18:33-37 shows his kingdom not of this world.

themes: 'Art thou the King of the Jews?'

schools.summary: The imperfect of repeated accusations, venom let loose in court; many MSS omit 'but he answered nothing' (implied); the charges magic, blasphemy, kingship, forbidding tribute (Gill).
Gill: Pilate asked again before the accusing priests, the former question put alone in the hall, the Jews not entering for fear of defilement (John 18:28).
Pilate sure of his innocence, seeing through their envy (v.10), marvelled at his self-control; the silence fulfilling Isaiah 53:7 (John 19:9).
The imperfect 'used to release' marks a regular Passover custom (John 18:39); Lightfoot and Gill argue against 'every feast', the release fitting the feast of Israel's deliverance.
Bede: Pilate furnished opportunities to release Jesus; the Jews chose a murderer and lost their salvation; Pseudo-Jerome reads the two goats (Leviticus 16:7-10) — Barabbas loosed, the Lord slain.
Barabbas a rebel leader and murderer, bound with the insurrectionists (Mark alone notes them); the very revolution the Jews wanted Jesus to lead (John 6:15); insurrection a crime Pilate punished.
The imperfect 'as he was wont to do' confirms the custom; Clarke: the word for 'crying aloud' marks the bellowing of the multitude, a loud and long cry.
The question implies a half-hope of yes (John 18:39); 'the King of the Jews' sharpens the contrast with Barabbas; Pilate satisfied of his innocence.
Wesley: the poor coward sacrifices justice to popular clamour, enraging the rulers by repeating the offensive title.
The imperfect 'he perceived' marks Pilate's dawning apprehension; both Mark and Matthew give envy as the motive (Proverbs 27:4; Acts 13:45), Pilate having heard of Christ's popularity.
The strong verb 'stirred up' (shook like an earthquake), where Matthew 27:20 has 'persuaded'; not the triumphal-entry crowd but a mob got together early; the rejection of the Holy One (Acts 3:14).

themes: the priests stir up the people

schools.summary: Pilate 'rubs it in', the 'then' meaning 'since you chose Barabbas'; the same determination to fasten the reproach of their king on them as in the cross-title (John 19:21-22).

outliers.note: The note here concerns the epileptic boy, not this verse.

themes: 'whom ye call the King of the Jews'

schools.summary: A chorus and hubbub of confused voices demanding crucifixion, some of whom had joined the triumphal-entry hallelujahs (Robertson); they had cried so before (Luke 23:21).
Gill: the third time Pilate spoke in Christ's defence.
Barnes: Pilate's 'what evil?' testifies to the innocence of the suffering servant (Isaiah 53:9).
'To content the multitude' a Latin idiom; Pilate afraid of the crowd and of Caesar yielded; the scourging preceding crucifixion.
Henry: he released Barabbas the nation's scandal and gave up Jesus its glory; chastising the innocent led by degrees to crucifying him.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): the smiting and spitting fulfil Isaiah 50:6 and 53:1.
'Praetorium' a Latin word for the governor's palace (Matthew 27:27); the 'band' the cohort gathered into the court.

Bede: mocking his kingship they robe him in purple and thorns; Pseudo-Jerome: his shame took away our shame, the thorny crown winning us the crown of the kingdom; Theophylact bids us put on the royal robe and crown of a strict life.
Mark/John 'purple', Matthew 'scarlet' (Matthew 27:28) — one near colour, a soldier's old coat; the crown a garland about the temples; Clarke (with Pearce) doubts the thorns were the torturing kind.
Gill: a mock purple robe, one of the soldiers' old coats, put on him in derision.

debates.question: Was the 'crown of thorns' an instrument of torture or of mockery?

sides.position: Chiefly mockery; the plant perhaps acanthus, not the torturing kind

themes: purple robe and crown of thorns

schools.summary: Gill: they saluted him in mockery, as a king just come to his throne on his coronation day.

outliers.note: The note here concerns the epileptic boy, not this verse.

schools.summary: The verbs smote, spat, worshipped in the tense of frequent repetition; the 'worship' mock homage, not adoration; the reed driving the thorns into his temples (Gill).
The vivid present 'they lead him out' after the imperfects of mockery; his own clothes restored for the road (Matthew 27:31); Augustine harmonises the myrrh/gall.

Pseudo-Jerome's types — Abel, Isaac with the wood, the serpent on the pole, the grape-cluster; Bede: Simon the Cyrenian (obedient, heir) the Gentiles bearing the cross; Theophylact: Christ bore his own cross (John 19:17) till Simon was compelled.

The Persian word for 'compel' (impress, Matthew 5:41); Simon coming from the country; the named sons Alexander and Rufus (peculiar to Mark) known to the readers, the Rufus of Romans 16:13 plausibly the same, linking Mark to Rome.
The historical present 'they bring him'; Golgotha 'the place of a skull' (Matthew 27:33); an infamous place of execution.

Henry: the common place of execution, numbering him with transgressors; the old tradition (Tertullian, Origen, Chrysostom) of Adam's grave there — as in Adam all die, in Christ all live (1 Corinthians 15:22).

Matthew's 'vinegar/gall' (Matthew 27:34) and Mark's 'wine mingled with myrrh' the same bitter drugged drink; 'myrrhed wine'; Jesus tasted but refused it; given by the Jews to the dying to dull the senses (Gill).
'What each should take' (double interrogative) only in Mark (Matthew 27:35-44); the parting of garments fulfilling Psalm 22:18; Mark seems to give the order nailing, parting, rearing (Wesley).

outliers.note: The note here is misplaced, not bearing on the verse.

schools.summary: 'The third hour' Jewish time (9 A.M.); the trial 'sixth hour' Roman (John 19:14); the day in four three-hour parts each named from its first hour (Barnes, Clarke); the precise hour peculiar to Mark.
Calvin: the day divided into four parts each named from its commencement; John reckoned by the larger division as noon approached.

debates.question: How is Mark's 'third hour' reconciled with John's 'sixth hour' (John 19:14)?

sides.position: Mark uses Jewish time (9 A.M.), John Roman time (6 A.M.)
The day in four quarters each named from its start; John reckons by the quarter

schools.summary: The 'superscription' (epigraph) on the cross, 'accusation' in Matthew 27:37, 'title' in John 19:19; Mark gives the shortest form; the cause of his death (Gill).
'Thieves' better 'robbers' (Matthew 27:38); the apocryphal names Zoathon and Chammatha (Clarke); crucified with him for greater reproach (Gill).

The verse wanting in nearly all the better MSS, probably a marginal note from Luke 22:37; Isaiah 53:12 means not that he was a transgressor but that he had a place with them, dying the just for the unjust.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): the two robbers — one a transgressor to the end, the other finding in his cross the beginning of restoration.

debates.question: Is verse 28 ('the scripture was fulfilled') part of the original Mark?

sides.position: No — wanting in the best MSS, likely a marginal note from Luke 22:37

schools.summary: The interjection 'Ah' (inarticulate scorn) peculiar to Mark; the head-wagging fulfilling Psalm 22:7; the taunt of the temple-saying.

Theophylact: the devil moved them to bid him come down, knowing salvation was won by the cross; Bede: they confess against their will he saved many; Pseudo-Jerome: more wonderful to rise from death than to come down living.
Ellicott: the clauses should read 'come down from the cross, and save thyself'; Gill: the taunt that, if he were what he claimed, he could free himself.

outliers.note: The note here concerns the second Passion prediction, not this verse.

schools.summary: Gill: the chief priests mocked, laughing at one another (Syriac), having gained their end and satiated their revenge.

'Now come down'; they use Christ's own language of seeing and believing in mockery (imperfect 'reproached'); Mark and Matthew omit the penitent robber (Luke 23:39-43); 'the Christ' with emphatic article.
'The sixth hour' noon (Jewish time), darkness till three (Matthew 27:45); no natural eclipse, the moon full at Passover (Theophylact); the sun down at noon (Amos 8:9).
Bede: the light withdrew lest it see the Lord hanging; as Adam sinned after midday, the second Adam by dying destroyed death; Henry: a sign of darkness on the Jewish nation.
Mark's Aramaic 'Eloi' a closer reproduction than Matthew's Hebrew 'Eli', both from Psalm 22:1 (Lamentations 1:12).
Wesley: claiming God as his God yet lamenting the Father's withdrawn tokens, treated as an enemy while bearing our sins.
They misunderstood the 'Eloi' (my God) for Elijah (Robertson); some of those standing by the cross (Gill); the Jews looked for Elijah (Malachi 4:5).
'To take him down' where Matthew 27:49 has 'to save him'; the vinegar fulfilling Psalm 69:21; Christ's 'I thirst' here (John 19:28).
Calvin harmonises: the jeer begun by one offering the vinegar, seized by others — no inconsistency with Matthew.

outliers.note: The note here is misplaced, not bearing on the verse.

schools.summary: 'Gave up the ghost' = breathed out (Matthew 27:50), the verb used again at v.39; about the ninth hour, when the paschal lamb was sacrificed (Clarke).
Pseudo-Jerome: men of earth die with a low voice, but he who came from heaven breathed his last with a loud voice — a mark of unspent strength.

Pseudo-Jerome: the veil rent = heaven opened; Theophylact: the Spirit departing, the Holy of Holies laid open, a figure of Christ's flesh torn (Hebrews 10:20); Bede and Augustine join the centurion's confession.
Gill: the rending from top to bottom with the earthquake and opening graves (Matthew 27:51).
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): the garden and the finished mystery.
'Centurion' a Latin word; the watching 'over against him' peculiar to Mark; the confession 'so' (with darkness and earthquake), perhaps 'a Son of God' (Matthew 27:54).
Bede: he marvelled that the Lord sent forth his Spirit, which only the Creator of souls can do; Augustine: the immediate yielding showed no sin could cause the death of his flesh.
The Cyril of Jerusalem note here is on the resurrection appearances, tangential.

outliers.note: A passage on the resurrection appearances, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The list matches Matthew 27:56, with 'James the little' and 'Salome' (only in Mark) for 'the mother of Zebedee's children'; they watched afar off.
The imperfects 'followed' and 'ministered' mark the long Galilean ministry of these women (Luke 8:1-3) who came up with him; last at the Cross; they provided his necessaries (Wesley).

'The preparation' explained by Mark for Gentiles as the day before the sabbath, our Friday (John 19:31); the rabbinic sense of preparing food (Exodus 16:23); haste before sunset, bodies not to hang overnight.
Bede: as man was made on the sixth day and God rested on the seventh, fitly the Saviour was crucified on the sixth and rested in the tomb on the sabbath, awaiting the eighth-day Resurrection.

outliers.note: The note here concerns the discourse on offences, not this verse.

schools.summary: A councillor of honourable estate (Sanhedrin member, rich, Matthew 27:57), looking for the kingdom (verb of Simeon, Luke 2:25), a secret disciple (John 19:38) now bold; he acts more fearlessly than the disciples (Clarke).
Calvin: the highest praise is that he waited for the kingdom of God, the root of his righteousness; Theophylact marks his praiseworthy boldness.
The classical idiom 'wonder if'; crucifixion usually lingering; this item and the calling of the centurion only in Mark; the article points to the same centurion of v.39.
'Granted the corpse' (ptoma, only here of Jesus' body); Pilate glad to give it to Joseph rather than the potter's field; the centurion in charge gave correct information (John 19:34).

The rare verbs for winding the body in linen, with Nicodemus's spices (John 19:39-40), laid in Joseph's new rock-hewn tomb; the buying of linen before the sabbath peculiar to Mark; the stone rolled by servants (Wesley).
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): the rock-hewn tomb foretold by the prophets.

themes: the stone rolled to the door

schools.summary: The imperfect 'beheld' — the two Marys sitting over against the tomb (Matthew 27:61), a party of observation seeing where he was laid; 'the other Mary' of Matthew.
Clarke: a timid man and weak women acknowledge Jesus when the strong forsook him; Barnes: female love never forsook him, flourishing in adversity.
